text: Hi everyone. Today's video is about an obese patient with a large abdominal wall incisional hernia. She had a previous midline laparotomy. The patient has been told to lose weight before such elective surgery could be offered to her.

Whilst waiting, she presented to the emergency department with an obstructed abdominal wall incisional hernia. This was a large CT proven obstructed abdominal wall hernia with a main bulk on the left side of the midline.

The surgeon inserts the first Visiport blunt entry in the right upper quadrant of the patient's abdomen, revealing a large fat apron and a relatively short abdomen. The hernia has been reduced from inside, providing a clear view of the surgical field.

The surgeon performs meticulous adhesiolysis to clear numerous adhesions within the abdomen. Fifteen centimeters of ischemic small bowel is released from the incarcerated incisional hernia. The hernia defect is identified as a tight five to six centimeter opening in the midline.

The surgeon places the small bowel, which is on a long loop of mesentery, into the pelvis. A small suprapubic incision is made in the lower abdomen, and the bowel is delivered out for a side-to-side resection anastomosis. The bowel is then replaced back into the abdomen using an Alexis wound retractor, ensuring there is no twist on the mesentery or bowel.

The surgeon observes the abdominal cavity, noting the absence of fecal or purulent contamination. A biodesign mesh is selected for repairing the hernia defect following the small bowel resection anastomosis.

The surgeon attempts to parachute the mesh onto the abdominal wall defect using a straight needle stitch, but due to the thickness of the abdominal wall, the needle does not penetrate. The mesh is lifted onto the defect and anchored with protac tuckers, aiming for a four to five centimeter overlap with the hernia edge. The superior edge of the defect lacks satisfactory cover due to a large fat adhesion.

The surgeon places a biodesign bio mesh at the superior edge of the hernia defect to achieve satisfactory coverage, noting the mesh's lack of strength and stiffness compared to composite or synthetic meshes.

The surgeon carefully places the mesh against the abdominal wall, ensuring a satisfactory cover despite the odd shape of the abdomen and the emergency nature of the repair. The mesh is positioned to cover the hernia defect completely.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic aspiration of a small bowel interloop collection, identified via CT scan, in a patient who returned to the operating room three days post-operation.
If you like my video, please hit the subscribe button. Thank you.
